ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for stir-fried instant noodles with seafood in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Maggi Noodle with Seafood is approximately 350 g, comprising a plate of stir-fried instant noodles topped with mixed seafood, egg, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Fried noodles with seafood (Mee goreng seafood) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo entry for stir-fried noodles with seafood used as the primary reference. Maggi instant noodles are the noodle base in this dish; the overall nutrient profile is consistent with HPB's fried noodle with seafood category. Values are per 100 g of the prepared dish. Minor adaptation noted: HPB entry covers yellow wheat noodles; Maggi noodles are a wheat-based instant noodle with comparable macro profile.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, cooked — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Maggi noodle stir-fry with seafood. GI of 47 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked instant noodles (GI ~47, low category), which is the primary noodle base of this dish. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 67.2) / 100 ≈ 31.6; however, adjusted to 25.6 using net available carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 19.2 g/100 g = 67.2 g carbohydrate; GL = 47 × 54.5 / 100 ≈ 25.6 using digestible carbohydrate estimate after fibre subtraction of 3.5 g). Stir-frying with protein and fat further moderates postprandial glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg/100 g; ~1680 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoning packets, which can contribute up to 70–84% of the recommended daily sodium limit (2000 mg) in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load with low GI noodle base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of instant noodles (GI ~47) moderates postprandial blood glucose rise, but the substantial carbohydrate load per serving (~67 g) and the presence of high-sodium sauces mean that portion control and monitoring of total carbohydrate intake remain important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from stir-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and seafood raise cardiovascular concern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sauce preparation and limit portion size to manage sodium and total fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maggi Noodle with Seafood (Mi Xao Hai San) is a popular stir-fried noodle dish served in Vietnamese-Singaporean restaurants and hawker-style eateries across Singapore. The dish uses Maggi brand instant wheat noodles as its base, which are first boiled until tender and then wok-tossed over high heat with a medley of seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish cake — along with egg, bean sprouts, cabbage, and spring onion. The sauce is built from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and garlic, giving the dish a savoury, umami-rich flavour with a slightly smoky wok-hei character. The texture contrasts soft, slightly chewy noodles with tender seafood and crisp vegetables. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~142 kcal/100 g), a reasonable protein contribution from seafood and egg (~6.8 g/100 g), and a low-GI carbohydrate base from instant noodles (GI ~47). However, sodium content is high (~480 mg/100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with shellfish, gluten, egg, or soy allergies. It is not vegetarian or vegan. In Singapore's multicultural food landscape, this dish bridges Vietnamese culinary tradition — reflected in its Vietnamese name Mì Xào Hải Sản — with the ubiquitous Maggi noodle brand familiar across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for Maggi Noodle with Seafood specifically. Values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo category for stir-fried noodles with seafood, which is the closest approved primary source match. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked instant noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
